stimulus: All constitutional amendments require ratification by three-fourths of the states. Some proposed amendments never achieve ratification. Therefore, some proposed amendments never become part of the Constitution. This argument moves from universal and particular premises to a particular conclusion.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: All published novels must pass editorial review. Some manuscripts never pass editorial review. Therefore, some manuscripts are never published as novels.
choice_b: All published novels must pass editorial review. This manuscript passed editorial review. Therefore, this manuscript will be published as a novel.
choice_c: Some published novels are bestsellers. All bestsellers receive marketing support. Therefore, some published novels receive marketing support.
choice_d: All published novels must pass editorial review. No self-published works pass editorial review. Therefore, no self-published works are published novels.
choice_e: Most published novels pass editorial review. This manuscript did not pass editorial review. Therefore, this manuscript will probably not be published.
explanation: The stimulus reasons: All A require B; some A lack B; therefore some A never achieve the status that requires B. Choice A mirrors this: All published novels require editorial review; some manuscripts never pass review; therefore some manuscripts are never published.
explanation_a: Correct. The structure matches: All A require B, some C never achieve B, therefore some C never become A. Both arguments combine a universal requirement with a particular negative to derive a particular conclusion.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent of the universal: All A require B, X has B, therefore X is A. Passing review does not guarantee publication.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This chains two premises differently: Some A are B, All B are C, therefore Some A are C. The stimulus uses All A require B and Some A lack B.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This uses a universal negative (No C are B) combined with the universal premise to derive a universal negative conclusion, which is a different structure from the particular conclusion in the stimulus.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This uses 'most' and 'probably,' making it an inductive argument rather than the deductive structure of the stimulus.

stimulus: No deep-sea fish can survive in freshwater. The anglerfish is a deep-sea fish. Therefore, the anglerfish cannot survive in freshwater. Furthermore, since some aquarium owners keep only freshwater tanks, those owners cannot keep anglerfish.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: No tropical plants can survive frost. The orchid is a tropical plant. Therefore, orchids cannot survive frost. Since some gardeners live in regions with harsh winters, those gardeners cannot grow orchids outdoors.
choice_b: All tropical plants need sunlight. Some gardeners do not provide enough sunlight. Therefore, some gardeners cannot grow tropical plants.
choice_c: No tropical plants can survive frost. Some plants survived the frost. Therefore, those plants are not tropical.
choice_d: Most tropical plants need warm climates. This greenhouse provides a warm climate. Therefore, most tropical plants can grow in this greenhouse.
choice_e: No tropical plants can survive frost. Therefore, any plant that survives frost is not tropical. This means that frost-hardy gardens contain no tropical plants.
explanation: The stimulus chains two inferences: (1) No A can do B, X is A, so X cannot do B; then (2) some people have only B-conditions, so those people cannot have X. Choice A follows this two-step pattern exactly.
explanation_a: Correct. Step 1: No A (tropical plants) survive B (frost), X (orchid) is A, so X cannot survive B. Step 2: Some people (gardeners in harsh winters) have B-conditions, so they cannot have X outdoors. Both steps match the stimulus.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This uses 'All' rather than 'No' and does not apply the rule to a specific species before generalizing to some group of people.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This uses modus tollens from the universal negative but does not include the second inference about a group of people who are restricted.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This uses 'most' and reasons about a specific location rather than following the two-step chain in the stimulus.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This restates the contrapositive and draws a conclusion about gardens, but it does not first apply the rule to a specific plant and then reason about a subset of people, as the stimulus does.

stimulus: Every society that has developed writing has also developed a system of laws. The ancient Sumerians developed writing. Therefore, the ancient Sumerians developed a system of laws. But since not all legal systems are just, it does not follow that Sumerian laws were just.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: Every country that industrializes experiences urbanization. Brazil industrialized. Therefore, Brazil experienced urbanization. But since not all urbanization leads to improved living standards, it does not follow that Brazil's urbanization improved living standards.
choice_b: Every country that industrializes experiences pollution. Some countries have not industrialized. Therefore, some countries have not experienced pollution.
choice_c: Every country that industrializes experiences economic growth. Japan experienced economic growth. Therefore, Japan industrialized.
choice_d: Most countries that industrialize experience income inequality. China industrialized. Therefore, China probably experiences income inequality.
choice_e: Every country that industrializes experiences urbanization. Not all urbanization is beneficial. Therefore, industrialization is not always beneficial.
explanation: The stimulus has three moves: (1) All A entails B, (2) X is A so X has B, (3) but not all B have property C, so X's B need not have C. Choice A replicates all three steps.
explanation_a: Correct. (1) All A (industrializing countries) experience B (urbanization). (2) X (Brazil) is A, so X experienced B. (3) Not all B leads to C (improved living standards), so X's B need not have C. This matches all three steps.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This draws a conclusion about countries that lack the antecedent property, using a different logical form than the stimulus.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent: All A leads to B, X has B, therefore X is A.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This uses 'most' and 'probably,' making it inductive rather than deductive with a qualification, as in the stimulus.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This draws a general evaluative conclusion about industrialization itself, rather than applying the rule to a specific case and then qualifying the conclusion about that case.

stimulus: If a novel employs an unreliable narrator, readers must carefully evaluate the narrator's claims. If readers must carefully evaluate claims, they engage more deeply with the text. Therefore, novels with unreliable narrators cause readers to engage more deeply. Since deep engagement produces lasting impressions, such novels produce lasting impressions.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: If a recipe uses exotic spices, the cook must source ingredients carefully. If the cook sources carefully, the meal will have authentic flavors. Therefore, recipes with exotic spices produce authentic flavors. Since authentic flavors delight diners, such recipes delight diners.
choice_b: If a recipe is complicated, it takes a long time. If something takes a long time, people avoid it. Therefore, complicated recipes are avoided. But some people enjoy cooking.
choice_c: If a recipe uses fresh ingredients, the dish is healthier. This dish is healthier. Therefore, this recipe uses fresh ingredients.
choice_d: Some recipes require special equipment. Special equipment is expensive. Therefore, some recipes are expensive to prepare.
choice_e: If a recipe is traditional, it has been tested over generations. Most traditional recipes produce reliable results. Therefore, most tested recipes produce reliable results.
explanation: The stimulus chains three conditionals via hypothetical syllogism and then extends with a fourth: A->B, B->C, so A->C; C->D, so A->D. Choice A follows this exact four-link chain.
explanation_a: Correct. A (exotic spices) -> B (source carefully), B -> C (authentic flavors), so A -> C; C -> D (delight diners), so A -> D. This is the same extended hypothetical syllogism.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This chains two conditionals but then adds a contradicting qualifier ('but some people enjoy cooking'), which is structurally different from the stimulus's fourth-link extension.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent rather than chaining conditionals.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This uses particular quantifiers ('some') and does not involve conditional chaining.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This mixes a universal conditional with 'most,' and the conclusion does not follow the chain structure of the stimulus.

stimulus: Some medications that are effective for adults are unsafe for children. Ibuprofen is effective for adults. Therefore, ibuprofen is unsafe for children. This reasoning is flawed because the fact that some members of a category have a property does not mean a particular member has that property.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its flawed reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: Most investments that yield high returns are volatile. This stock yields high returns. Therefore, this stock is probably volatile.
choice_b: All investments that yield high returns carry significant risk. This mutual fund yields high returns. Therefore, this mutual fund carries significant risk.
choice_c: Some investments are risky. Some risky things should be avoided. Therefore, some investments should be avoided.
choice_d: No safe investments yield high returns. Government bonds are safe investments. Therefore, government bonds do not yield high returns.
choice_e: Some investments that yield high returns carry significant risk. Municipal bonds yield high returns. Therefore, municipal bonds carry significant risk.
explanation: The stimulus's flaw: Some A that are B are also C. X is B. Therefore, X is C. This treats 'some' as though it applies to all or to a specific case. Choice A commits the same error.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This uses 'most' and hedges with 'probably,' making it a reasonable inductive argument rather than the illicit move from 'some' to a specific case.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This uses 'all' rather than 'some,' making it a valid deductive argument with no flaw.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This chains two 'some' statements, which is a different structure. It does not single out a specific member and attribute a property from 'some.'
explanation_d: Incorrect. This uses a universal negative and is a valid syllogism. No flaw is present.
explanation_e: Correct. Some A (high-return investments) are C (risky). X (municipal bonds) is A. Therefore X is C. This treats 'some' as applying to a specific member, just like the stimulus.

stimulus: The right to free speech does not protect speech that incites violence. Henderson's speech incited violence. Therefore, Henderson's speech is not protected by the right to free speech. Since unprotected speech can be legally restricted, Henderson's speech can be legally restricted.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: Most warranties cover mechanical failures. This appliance had a mechanical failure. Therefore, this failure is probably covered by the warranty.
choice_b: The warranty covers all manufacturing defects. This product has a defect. Therefore, this defect is covered by the warranty.
choice_c: The warranty does not cover cosmetic damage. Therefore, cosmetic damage should not be reported to the manufacturer.
choice_d: The warranty does not cover damage caused by misuse. The customer's damage was caused by misuse. Therefore, the customer's damage is not covered by the warranty. Since uncovered damage must be repaired at the owner's expense, the customer must pay for the repair.
choice_e: The warranty does not cover accidental damage. Some damage is accidental. Therefore, some damage is not covered.
explanation: The stimulus uses a two-step argument: (1) Rule excludes X, case fits exclusion, so case is excluded; (2) excluded cases have consequence, so case has consequence. Choice A mirrors both steps.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This uses 'most' and 'probably,' making it inductive. The stimulus is deductive throughout.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This applies a positive coverage rule rather than an exclusion rule. It also lacks the second inferential step about consequences of exclusion.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This jumps from non-coverage to a recommendation about reporting, which introduces an unjustified normative conclusion not present in the stimulus's structure.
explanation_d: Correct. (1) Warranty excludes misuse damage, customer's damage is from misuse, so not covered. (2) Uncovered damage must be paid by owner, so customer pays. Both steps match the stimulus.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This draws a general particular conclusion (some damage is not covered) rather than applying the rule to a specific case and then deriving a further consequence for that case.

stimulus: Whenever a glacier retreats, the exposed land undergoes ecological succession. The Mendenhall Glacier has been retreating for decades. Therefore, the land exposed by the Mendenhall Glacier has been undergoing ecological succession. This reasoning treats a general ecological principle as applicable to a specific instance.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: Whenever a volcano erupts, it releases ash into the atmosphere. Ash in the atmosphere cools the planet. Therefore, volcanic eruptions cool the planet.
choice_b: Some glaciers are retreating due to climate change. The Mendenhall Glacier is retreating. Therefore, the Mendenhall Glacier is retreating due to climate change.
choice_c: Whenever a star exhausts its hydrogen fuel, it expands into a red giant. The sun will eventually exhaust its hydrogen fuel. Therefore, the sun will eventually expand into a red giant.
choice_d: Most coral reefs are threatened by ocean acidification. The Great Barrier Reef is a coral reef. Therefore, the Great Barrier Reef is definitely threatened by ocean acidification.
choice_e: Whenever an earthquake occurs along a fault line, the fault shifts. This fault has shifted. Therefore, an earthquake occurred along it.
explanation: The stimulus applies a universal principle to a specific case: Whenever A happens, B follows; A is happening in case X; therefore B is happening in case X. Choice A does the same.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This chains two universal principles (A causes B, B causes C, therefore A causes C), which is hypothetical syllogism, not simple application to a specific case.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This uses 'some' and attributes a specific cause to a specific case, which is the some-to-specific fallacy rather than a valid application of a universal principle.
explanation_c: Correct. Whenever A (hydrogen exhaustion) occurs, B (red giant expansion) follows. The sun will undergo A. Therefore, the sun will undergo B. This is modus ponens applied to a specific astronomical object, matching the stimulus.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This uses 'most' but draws a definite conclusion ('definitely'), which is a quantifier error. The stimulus uses 'whenever,' which is universal.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent: Whenever A then B, B occurred, therefore A occurred.

stimulus: If economic inequality increases, then social unrest will follow. If social unrest follows, then the government will implement reforms. Economic inequality has not increased. Therefore, the government will not implement reforms. This argument treats the absence of one cause as eliminating all paths to the effect.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its flawed reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: If deforestation continues, biodiversity will decline. Biodiversity has declined. Therefore, deforestation has continued.
choice_b: If deforestation continues, biodiversity will decline. If biodiversity declines, ecosystems will collapse. Deforestation has not continued. Therefore, ecosystems will not collapse.
choice_c: If deforestation continues, biodiversity will decline. If biodiversity declines, ecosystems will collapse. Ecosystems have not collapsed. Therefore, deforestation has not continued.
choice_d: If deforestation continues, biodiversity will decline. Deforestation has continued. Therefore, biodiversity will decline.
choice_e: If deforestation continues, biodiversity will decline. If pollution increases, biodiversity will also decline. Neither deforestation nor pollution has occurred. Therefore, biodiversity will not decline.
explanation: The stimulus denies the antecedent in a chain: A->B->C, not A, therefore not C. This is flawed because C could occur through other means. Choice A commits the same error.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent of a single conditional, which is a different flaw.
explanation_b: Correct. A (deforestation) -> B (biodiversity decline) -> C (ecosystem collapse), not A, therefore not C. This denies the antecedent of a conditional chain, just like the stimulus, ignoring that ecosystems could collapse for other reasons.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This is valid modus tollens applied through the chain: not C, therefore not B, therefore not A. This is logically correct, not flawed.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This is valid modus ponens. No flaw is present.
explanation_e: Incorrect. While this also denies antecedents, it explicitly addresses two possible causes, making its structure different. The stimulus and the correct answer deny only one antecedent in a chain.

stimulus: All Renaissance paintings in this collection use oil on canvas. No watercolors use oil on canvas. Therefore, no watercolors in this collection are Renaissance paintings. The conclusion follows validly from the two premises about the collection.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: All organic foods are pesticide-free. All pesticide-free foods are healthy. Therefore, all organic foods are healthy.
choice_b: All organic foods are healthy. Some healthy foods are expensive. Therefore, some organic foods are expensive.
choice_c: All organic foods are pesticide-free. This food is pesticide-free. Therefore, this food is organic.
choice_d: Some organic foods are locally grown. Some locally grown foods are affordable. Therefore, some organic foods are affordable.
choice_e: All organic foods in this store are pesticide-free. No conventionally grown foods are pesticide-free. Therefore, no conventionally grown foods in this store are organic.
explanation: The stimulus combines a universal affirmative with a universal negative: All A are B, No C are B, therefore No C are A. Choice A follows this exact syllogistic form.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This is a valid hypothetical syllogism (All A are B, All B are C, therefore All A are C), but it uses two universal affirmatives rather than a universal affirmative and a universal negative.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This uses All A are B and Some B are C to conclude Some A are C, which is actually an invalid syllogism (undistributed middle). Different structure from the stimulus.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent of a universal: All A are B, X is B, therefore X is A.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This chains two 'some' statements, which cannot validly yield a conclusion. It is a different and invalid structure.
explanation_e: Correct. All A (organic foods in store) are B (pesticide-free), No C (conventionally grown) are B, therefore No C are A. This is the same syllogistic structure.

stimulus: The defendant claims he was not at the scene of the crime because he was dining at a restaurant. The restaurant's security footage shows no one matching the defendant's description that evening. Therefore, the defendant's alibi is false, and he must have been at the scene. This reasoning assumes that if the alibi is disproven, the only remaining possibility is guilt.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its flawed reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: All witnesses confirm the car was red. The defendant claims the car was blue. Therefore, the defendant is not credible.
choice_b: The politician claims she supports education reform because she voted for the bill. The voting record confirms she voted for the bill. Therefore, she supports education reform.
choice_c: The student claims he completed the assignment on time. The timestamp shows it was submitted late. Therefore, the student is lying about completing it on time.
choice_d: The company claims the product is safe because it passed laboratory testing. Independent analysis found flaws in the testing methodology. Therefore, the product must be unsafe.
choice_e: The scientist claims the experiment succeeded. The data shows the hypothesis was confirmed. Therefore, the experiment succeeded.
explanation: The stimulus's flaw: disproving the reason offered for X does not disprove X itself, nor does it prove the opposite. The defendant's alibi being false does not prove he was at the scene. Choice A mirrors this: disproving the basis for a safety claim does not prove the product is unsafe.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This uses witness testimony to impeach credibility, which is a reasonable inference, not the same structural flaw.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This confirms the stated evidence and draws a reasonable conclusion. No flaw is present.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This directly disproves the specific claim (submitted on time) with contradicting evidence (late timestamp). This is a valid rebuttal, not the same flaw as in the stimulus.
explanation_d: Correct. Disproving the evidence for a claim (flawed testing methodology) is treated as proof that the opposite is true (product is unsafe). This mirrors the stimulus's flaw of treating a disproven alibi as proof of presence at the scene.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This confirms a claim with supporting evidence. No flaw is present.

stimulus: Every element in Group 1 of the periodic table is a metal. Lithium is in Group 1. Therefore, lithium is a metal. Every metal conducts electricity. Therefore, lithium conducts electricity.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: Every member of the orchestra plays an instrument. Every person who plays an instrument practices daily. Therefore, every member of the orchestra practices daily.
choice_b: Some members of the orchestra play strings. Yuki plays strings. Therefore, Yuki is a member of the orchestra.
choice_c: Every member of the orchestra plays an instrument. Yuki is a member of the orchestra. Therefore, Yuki plays an instrument. Every person who plays an instrument reads music. Therefore, Yuki reads music.
choice_d: Every member of the orchestra plays an instrument. Not everyone who plays an instrument is in the orchestra. Therefore, some instrumentalists are not in the orchestra.
choice_e: Most members of the orchestra play strings. Yuki is in the orchestra. Therefore, Yuki probably plays strings.
explanation: The stimulus applies universal instantiation twice: All A are B, X is A, so X is B; All B are C, so X is C. Choice A does the same two-step instantiation.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This chains two universals without applying them to a specific individual. It derives a universal conclusion (All A are C) rather than a conclusion about a particular person.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent using 'some': Some A are B, X is B, therefore X is A.
explanation_c: Correct. All A (orchestra members) are B (instrument players), X (Yuki) is A, so X is B. All B are C (read music), so X is C. This is the same two-step application of universal rules to a specific individual.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This combines a universal affirmative with a qualified negation and draws a particular conclusion about a group, not about a specific individual.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This uses 'most' and 'probably,' making it inductive rather than deductive.

stimulus: Three out of five members of the hiring committee preferred Candidate A. Since the committee decides by majority vote, Candidate A will be selected. However, since the committee only evaluates qualifications, and workplace success depends on many other factors, being selected does not guarantee Candidate A will succeed in the role.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: Four out of six jurors initially voted to convict. Since a conviction requires unanimity, the defendant will not be convicted based on the initial vote. However, since deliberations may change minds, the final outcome remains uncertain.
choice_b: Three out of five critics gave the film a positive review. Since the film is evaluated by majority critical opinion, it is considered a success. However, since critical opinion only measures artistic merit, and commercial success depends on marketing and timing, being well-reviewed does not guarantee box office success.
choice_c: All five judges scored the gymnast above 9.0. Therefore, the gymnast will win the competition. Since scoring is subjective, the result may be controversial.
choice_d: Two out of five board members support the merger. Therefore, the merger will not proceed. Since the merger would have been profitable, the board made a poor decision.
choice_e: Most voters support the referendum. Therefore, the referendum will pass. Since referendums are binding, the government must implement the result.
explanation: The stimulus has three elements: (1) majority within a group favors X, (2) majority rule means X wins, (3) but winning on one criterion does not guarantee success on a broader criterion. Choice B matches all three.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This reaches the opposite conclusion (will NOT be convicted) because unanimity is required, and then adds uncertainty about future deliberations. The structure differs from the stimulus's majority-wins-but-limited-scope pattern.
explanation_b: Correct. (1) Majority of critics are positive, (2) majority opinion means the film is considered a success, (3) but critical success does not guarantee commercial success because the evaluation criterion is limited. All three steps match.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This uses unanimity rather than majority, and the qualification (subjectivity) concerns the validity of the score rather than the gap between one type of success and another.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This reaches a negative conclusion (merger won't proceed) because the majority opposes it, then adds an evaluative judgment. The stimulus's qualification is about the limits of the criterion, not about the wisdom of the decision.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This draws a further consequence of winning (government must implement) rather than qualifying that winning on one criterion does not guarantee a broader success.

stimulus: In the Romantic era, poets who embraced emotion over reason produced works of enduring power. Keats embraced emotion over reason. Therefore, Keats produced works of enduring power. But Keats was not the only Romantic poet, so we cannot conclude that all Romantic poets produced works of enduring power.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: In the Baroque era, composers wrote complex fugues. Bach was a Baroque composer. Therefore, Bach wrote complex fugues. Bach is widely regarded as the greatest Baroque composer.
choice_b: In the Renaissance, artists who mastered perspective created visually stunning works. Da Vinci mastered perspective. Therefore, Da Vinci created visually stunning works. But Da Vinci was not the only Renaissance artist, so we cannot conclude that all Renaissance artists created visually stunning works.
choice_c: In the Classical era, some composers favored simplicity. Mozart was a Classical composer. Therefore, Mozart favored simplicity.
choice_d: In the Impressionist era, painters who used light effects were innovative. Monet used light effects. Therefore, all Impressionist painters were innovative.
choice_e: In the Modernist era, writers experimented with form. Joyce was a Modernist writer. Therefore, Joyce experimented with form. Since experimentation is valuable, Joyce's work is valuable.
explanation: The stimulus: (1) applies a conditional to a specific individual to derive a conclusion, then (2) explicitly cautions against generalizing from that individual to the entire group. Choice A follows both steps.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This adds an evaluative superlative rather than a caution against overgeneralization.
explanation_b: Correct. (1) Artists who mastered X produced Y, Da Vinci mastered X, so Da Vinci produced Y. (2) But Da Vinci was not the only artist, so we cannot generalize to all. Both steps match the stimulus.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This uses 'some' in the premise, making it a flawed inference (some-to-specific) rather than the valid inference followed by a caution that the stimulus employs.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This actually commits the overgeneralization that the stimulus warns against, concluding 'all' from a specific case.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This adds a value judgment as a further conclusion rather than cautioning against generalization.

stimulus: If a defendant waives the right to a jury trial, the case is decided by a judge. If the case is decided by a judge, the verdict relies on legal precedent rather than popular sentiment. Davis waived the right to a jury trial. Therefore, Davis's verdict will rely on legal precedent rather than popular sentiment.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: If a patient chooses surgery, recovery is lengthy. If a patient chooses medication, side effects may occur. Mrs. Chen chose one of these options. Therefore, Mrs. Chen faces either lengthy recovery or possible side effects.
choice_b: If a patient chooses surgery, the patient must undergo anesthesia. Mrs. Chen underwent anesthesia. Therefore, Mrs. Chen chose surgery.
choice_c: If a patient chooses surgery, recovery takes several weeks. If recovery takes several weeks, the patient misses work. Mrs. Chen did not miss work. Therefore, Mrs. Chen did not choose surgery.
choice_d: If a patient refuses treatment, the condition may worsen. If the condition worsens, hospitalization becomes necessary. Most patients do not refuse treatment. Therefore, most patients avoid hospitalization.
choice_e: If a patient chooses surgery, the patient must undergo anesthesia. If the patient undergoes anesthesia, the patient will be unconscious during the procedure. Mrs. Chen chose surgery. Therefore, Mrs. Chen will be unconscious during the procedure.
explanation: The stimulus chains two conditionals and then applies them to a specific case via modus ponens: A->B, B->C, X did A, therefore X gets C. Choice A follows this pattern exactly.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This presents a disjunction between two conditional paths rather than chaining one path forward.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent of the first conditional.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This uses modus tollens through the chain (not C, therefore not B, therefore not A), which is valid but structurally different from the stimulus's forward chain.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This uses 'most' and 'may,' introducing probabilistic reasoning rather than the deductive chain in the stimulus.
explanation_e: Correct. A (surgery) -> B (anesthesia) -> C (unconscious during procedure). X (Mrs. Chen) chose A. Therefore X gets C. This is hypothetical syllogism applied to a specific individual, matching the stimulus.

stimulus: Researchers found that students who listened to classical music while studying scored higher on exams. Professor Lin therefore requires her students to listen to classical music during study sessions, claiming this will raise their scores. But the original study only showed a correlation; the higher scores might have resulted from the type of student who chooses to listen to classical music, not from the music itself.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its flawed reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: A study found that bilingual children perform better on cognitive tests. The school added a language program. Test scores improved the following year.
choice_b: A study found that all patients who took the drug recovered. Dr. Shah therefore prescribed the drug to his patient, expecting recovery. The patient recovered.
choice_c: A study found that exercise reduces anxiety. Maria therefore began exercising and found her anxiety decreased. Maria concluded the exercise helped.
choice_d: A study found that employees who take regular breaks are more productive. The company therefore mandated regular breaks, expecting productivity to increase. But employees who voluntarily take breaks may already be better at self-regulation, and it is this trait, not the breaks themselves, that drives productivity.
choice_e: A study found that smokers have higher rates of lung cancer. The government therefore banned smoking in public places, reasoning that this would reduce lung cancer rates.
explanation: The stimulus identifies a correlation-to-causation flaw where a policy is based on a correlational finding, ignoring that a confounding variable (self-selection) could explain the result. Choice A parallels this exactly.
explanation_a: Incorrect. While this could involve correlation-causation issues, the description does not highlight the confounding variable or self-selection problem that is central to the stimulus's flaw.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This is a straightforward application of a finding where the outcome confirmed the expectation. The study found universal recovery (all patients), not just a correlation.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This describes a single individual's experience that confirmed the study. While not rigorous, it does not show the same institutional policy-from-correlation error.
explanation_d: Correct. A correlational finding (breaks correlate with productivity) is used to justify a causal policy (mandate breaks), while the actual cause may be a confounding variable (self-regulation traits). This mirrors the stimulus's flaw precisely.
explanation_e: Incorrect. While smoking and lung cancer involve a causal claim derived from correlation, the scientific consensus strongly supports causation here. More importantly, the structure does not highlight a self-selection confound.

stimulus: No valid scientific theory is untestable. String theory, some physicists argue, is currently untestable. If those physicists are correct, then string theory is not a valid scientific theory. This argument's conclusion is conditional on the truth of a disputed claim.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: All ethical business practices are transparent. Some business practices are not transparent. Therefore, some business practices are not ethical.
choice_b: No ethical business practice is deceptive. Targeted advertising is a business practice. Therefore, targeted advertising is not deceptive.
choice_c: No ethical business practice is deceptive. Some consumer advocates argue that targeted advertising is deceptive. If those advocates are correct, then targeted advertising is not an ethical business practice.
choice_d: No ethical business practice is deceptive. Targeted advertising is deceptive. Therefore, targeted advertising is not an ethical business practice.
choice_e: Most ethical business practices are transparent. Targeted advertising is not transparent. Therefore, targeted advertising is probably not ethical.
explanation: The stimulus: No A is B. Some argue X is B. If they are correct, X is not A. The conclusion is conditional on a disputed premise. Choice A mirrors this exactly.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This uses a universal affirmative and a particular negative to draw a particular conclusion, which is a different syllogistic form. It also does not have a conditional conclusion.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This draws a conclusion about advertising being not deceptive from its being a business practice, which does not follow from the premise (the premise says ethical practices are not deceptive, not that all practices are ethical).
explanation_c: Correct. No A (ethical practices) is B (deceptive). Some argue X (targeted advertising) is B. If correct, X is not A. The conclusion is conditional, matching the stimulus's structure.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This is a straightforward modus tollens without any conditional hedging. The stimulus's key feature is that the conclusion depends on a disputed claim.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This uses 'most' and 'probably,' making it inductive. The stimulus is deductive with a conditional conclusion.

stimulus: The tragic hero in classical drama always possesses a fatal flaw. Since Oedipus is a tragic hero in classical drama, Oedipus possesses a fatal flaw. The fact that Oedipus possesses a fatal flaw does not diminish his nobility; rather, it is precisely what makes his downfall so moving.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: The epic poem always features a hero's journey. The Odyssey features a hero's journey. Therefore, the Odyssey is an epic poem.
choice_b: The sonnet form always contains fourteen lines. Since Shakespeare's 'Sonnet 18' is a sonnet, it contains fourteen lines. The constraint of fourteen lines does not limit expressiveness; rather, it is precisely what gives the form its power.
choice_c: Most novels contain a central conflict. This novel contains a central conflict. Therefore, it is a well-written novel.
choice_d: The sonnet form always contains fourteen lines. Some modern poems contain fourteen lines. Therefore, some modern poems are sonnets.
choice_e: The tragic hero often experiences a reversal of fortune. Hamlet experiences a reversal. Therefore, Hamlet is a tragic hero.
explanation: The stimulus: (1) All A have B, X is A, so X has B (universal instantiation); (2) an evaluative commentary that B does not diminish but actually enhances a quality. Choice A follows both steps.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent: All A have B, X has B, therefore X is A.
explanation_b: Correct. (1) All A (sonnets) have B (fourteen lines), X (Sonnet 18) is A, so X has B. (2) B does not limit but actually enhances a quality. Both the deductive step and the evaluative commentary match.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This uses 'most' and draws an evaluative conclusion (well-written) that is not supported by the premise. The stimulus's evaluation is about how the property functions, not about quality.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This commits the fallacy of affirming the consequent with a quantifier: All A have B, some C have B, therefore some C are A.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This uses 'often' rather than 'always' and then affirms the consequent to classify Hamlet.

stimulus: The architect claims the building's design is structurally sound because each individual component passed stress testing. But the fact that each component is individually sound does not establish that the assembled structure is sound. Interactions between components can introduce weaknesses not present in any single part.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its flawed reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: The teacher claims every student understands the material because the class average was high. But a high average can mask the struggles of individual students.
choice_b: The manager claims the project will succeed because the budget is sufficient. But many projects with sufficient budgets still fail due to poor planning.
choice_c: The scientist claims the experiment is valid because the hypothesis was confirmed. But confirmation of a hypothesis does not prove the experimental design was sound.
choice_d: All ingredients in the recipe are fresh. Therefore, the dish will taste good. But good taste also depends on cooking technique.
choice_e: The coach claims the team will win the championship because every player on the roster is individually talented. But the fact that each player is talented does not establish that the team will perform well together. Team chemistry and coordination can undermine individual talent.
explanation: The stimulus identifies a composition fallacy: properties of individual parts do not necessarily transfer to the whole, because interactions between parts matter. Choice A commits the same error.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This is a whole-to-part fallacy (the reverse of the stimulus): a property of the whole (high average) is assumed to apply to each part (each student).
explanation_b: Incorrect. This is about a single factor (budget) being insufficient to guarantee success, not about individual parts being sound but the whole being unsound. There is no composition fallacy.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This confuses confirmation of results with validity of methods, which is a different type of reasoning error.
explanation_d: Incorrect. While this involves parts (ingredients) and a whole (dish), the key element of interactions between parts creating emergent weaknesses is replaced by a separate factor (technique), making it a different structure.
explanation_e: Correct. Individual players being talented (parts having a property) does not mean the team will win (whole has a corresponding property), because interactions (chemistry, coordination) can undermine the result. This is the same part-to-whole composition fallacy.

stimulus: Either the proposed regulation will reduce emissions or it will impose undue costs on businesses. If it reduces emissions, it achieves its environmental goal. If it imposes undue costs, it will face legal challenges. Therefore, the regulation will either achieve its environmental goal or face legal challenges.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: Either it will rain or it will be sunny. It will not rain. Therefore, it will be sunny.
choice_b: Either the stock market will rise or it will fall. If it rises, investors profit. Therefore, investors will profit.
choice_c: Either the candidate will win or lose. If the candidate wins, the policy will change. If the candidate loses, nothing will change. The candidate will probably win.
choice_d: Either the new drug will cure the disease or it will produce side effects. If it cures the disease, it will be approved by regulators. If it produces side effects, it will require further testing. Therefore, the drug will either be approved by regulators or require further testing.
choice_e: Either the merger will proceed or it will be blocked. If it proceeds, shareholders benefit. If it is blocked, competitors benefit. Therefore, someone will benefit, but it is unclear who.
explanation: The stimulus uses constructive dilemma: A or B; if A then C; if B then D; therefore C or D. Choice A follows this exact structure.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This is a simple disjunctive syllogism, not a constructive dilemma.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This presents a disjunction but only applies one conditional, ignoring the second disjunct entirely.
explanation_c: Incorrect. This presents the constructive dilemma setup but then adds a probability judgment rather than deriving the disjunctive conclusion.
explanation_d: Correct. A or B (cure or side effects), A->C (cure->approval), B->D (side effects->further testing), therefore C or D (approved or further testing). This is constructive dilemma, matching the stimulus.
explanation_e: Incorrect. While similar, the conclusion is vague ('someone will benefit, unclear who') rather than a precise disjunction of the two specific consequents.

stimulus: All amphibians undergo metamorphosis during development. No reptiles undergo metamorphosis. The axolotl undergoes metamorphosis. Therefore, the axolotl is an amphibian and not a reptile. This conclusion relies on the fact that the two categories are defined by mutually exclusive developmental traits.
stem: Which one of the following arguments is most similar in its reasoning to the argument above?
choice_a: All fish live in water. Dolphins live in water. Therefore, dolphins are fish.
choice_b: All mammals are warm-blooded. Some reptiles are warm-blooded. The platypus is warm-blooded. Therefore, the platypus is a mammal.
choice_c: All deciduous trees shed their leaves seasonally. No evergreens shed their leaves seasonally. The sugar maple sheds its leaves seasonally. Therefore, the sugar maple is a deciduous tree and not an evergreen.
choice_d: Most birds can fly. Penguins cannot fly. Therefore, penguins are not birds.
choice_e: All insects have six legs. Spiders have eight legs. Therefore, spiders are probably not insects.
explanation: The stimulus uses a universal affirmative and a universal negative about the same property to classify a specific entity: All A have P, No B have P, X has P, therefore X is A and not B. Choice A follows this exact structure.
explanation_a: Incorrect. This affirms the consequent: All A have P, X has P, therefore X is A. It lacks the universal negative that would rule out other categories.
explanation_b: Incorrect. This introduces 'some reptiles are warm-blooded,' which breaks the mutual exclusivity. The stimulus relies on the property being exclusive to one category.
explanation_c: Correct. All A (deciduous trees) have P (shed leaves), No B (evergreens) have P, X (sugar maple) has P, therefore X is A and not B. This uses the same dual-universal classification as the stimulus.
explanation_d: Incorrect. This uses 'most' rather than 'all,' and denies a property to exclude from a category, which is a different (and flawed) structure.
explanation_e: Incorrect. This uses a universal affirmative and a specific negation to tentatively exclude ('probably not'), hedging the conclusion. The stimulus draws a definite conclusion based on two universals.
